sources_audited.note: The cited Census PDF timed out on both reopening attempts, so its 621112 definition and cross-references could not be independently confirmed in this audit. The affected e and o inputs are already labeled ESTIMATE, and S7 independently supports the cited psychiatrist duties and licensing context used for o.

The May 2023 BLS page confirms 2,842,350 jobs and the cited 45.24%, 18.14%, and 26.73% major-group employment shares for broad NAICS 621100. It does not measure wage-weighted task exposure or exact-industry 621112 workflows, as the packet discloses.

The AMA page confirms 66% any-use adoption in 2024 versus 38% in 2023, the cited 10-21% task-use rates, and the stated privacy, integration, training, oversight, accuracy, and liability concerns. It does not directly support the numerical a or five-year rho estimates for LMM psychiatry practices.

The AMA page confirms private-practice participation fell from 60.1% in 2012 to 42.2% in 2024, hospital ownership increased, private-equity-owned practice participation rose from 4.5% to 6.5% in 2022-2024, and financial and administrative pressures motivated sales. These physician-wide ownership statistics do not directly measure qualifying firm transfers or exact-industry eligibility.

CMS confirms service-code payment amounts and calculation from work, practice-expense, and malpractice RVUs with geographic adjustment. It supports the fee-schedule mechanism but provides no evidence for the estimated share of AI benefit retained by psychiatry operators.

BLS confirms broad Offices of Physicians chained-2017-dollar output of $646.9 billion in 2024 and $893.9 billion in 2034, with 3.3% annual growth. The square-root five-year transformation is reproducible, but the source is not exact-621112 constant-quality demand.

BLS confirms psychiatrist duties, physician licensing, projected psychiatrist employment of 27,100 in 2024 and 28,800 in 2034, and its statement that growing psychiatric-care demand supports job growth. It does not directly quantify exact-industry five-year demand or the operator-required share of the service basket.

caveats: The cited Census manual could not be reopened because the PDF timed out twice; the exact 621112 definition and cross-references therefore remain unaudited in this review.

The a estimate is built from broad physician-office employment shares and physician-wide AI task use rather than a psychiatry-specific wage-weighted task study; its numerical base is judgment despite an explicit proxy bridge and wide range.

The rho base extrapolates five-year implementation depth from physician-wide incidence of any AI use and task-use percentages; the source does not measure autonomous completion, workflow coverage, or labor hours avoided in LMM psychiatry groups.

The s5 proxy uses physician ownership-setting changes as evidence of transfer activity, but those changes are not unique firm control transactions and are neither psychiatry-specific nor tied to the eligible-firm denominator.

CMS supports fee-for-service payment mechanics but not the q retention estimate; realized capture could differ materially with payer mix, clinician compensation, implementation cost, and whether time savings create capacity rather than removable payroll.

The d5 proxy applies broad Offices of Physicians real-output growth to psychiatry, while psychiatrist employment and a qualitative demand statement are supporting context rather than an exact constant-quality demand measure.

The lens marks the industry as non-heterogeneous even though medication-management, psychotherapy, ownership, payer, and staffing mixes can materially alter a, rho, q, and o; the packet acknowledges much of this variation in primitive caveats and ranges, so the flag does not alter the base tier or action.
summary: Mechanics pass and the record is useful as a model-conditioned screening result. Reopened sources substantiate the cited broad occupation, adoption, ownership, payment, output, and psychiatrist facts, while the packet honestly labels the score-driving transformations as proxies or estimates and discloses their main error directions. The Census PDF was not reachable during review, and no source directly measures psychiatry-specific automation exposure, implementation depth, qualifying transfers, benefit retention, or operator-required demand. Those weaknesses warrant caveats but do not constitute a material defect under the required base-tier/action test.
